_note: Hand-authored external figures used by the essay. kind: verified | illustrative. Every entry carries its source.

funding_cut_pct.label: real-terms cut in council core funding per resident since 2010-11

regressive.label: funding fell over twice as far in the poorest tenth of areas as in the richest — austerity by design

efs.label: emergency central support to councils in 2024/25, up from £79.9m in 2018/19 — a system on life support

care_leakage.label: a year leaks out of the UK care-home sector as profit, debt interest, rent and fees (~10% of income) — the market Devon's £3.5bn buys into

temp_accommodation.label: England's temporary-accommodation bill in 2024/25 (+118% in five years); councils' own unsubsidised share rose from 7% to 51%

exeter_city_living.label: delivered by Exeter's for-sale development company before it was wound up — £4.5m+ losses, ~£10.1m owed, against a 675-home target. The cautionary local lesson: build to rent (HRA), not to sell.

preston.label: extra anchor-institution spend redirected into Preston's local economy (5% → 18.2%)

cmi.label: raised directly from residents via local climate bonds across 8 councils since 2020 — cheaper than PWLB, but modest

land_value.label: of land-value uplift on new development goes uncaptured; >£8bn in developer contributions sits unspent

cautionary.label: debt at Woking and losses at Thurrock from speculative commercial bets — why borrowing to buy assets 'primarily for yield' was banned in 2020. Productive assets yes; financial speculation no.

cost_per_home.source: Housing Forum 2024 (new build, ex-land); Exeter's Passivhaus homes ~£393k each

avg_permanent_post.source: loaded permanent post; agency staff cost ~+53% more (DfE 2020)

agency_premium_pct.source: DfE 2020, children's social workers (via Community Care); 20–60% sensitivity band

contractor_margin_pct.source: Illustrative — APSE: outsourced management fees ~12–19%, up to ~30% recaptured (leisure case)

horizon_years.source: Illustrative horizon; matches the rent-vs-build chart

rent_plus_ta.label: rent + homelessness cost avoided
rent_plus_ta.source: (£5,000 rent + £21,700 TA avoided) ÷ £250,000 (Shelter/MHCLG 2024/25) — blends cash revenue with avoided cost

redirect_assumptions.note: Illustrative scenario, not a forecast. The saving is the recovered contractor margin/premium, not the gross spend. The HRA is ring-fenced from the General Fund (gov.uk), so savings fund homes indirectly — freeing capacity to lend/grant the HRA — not by direct transfer.

reinvest_options.label: Municipal solar generation
reinvest_options.note: ~4% cash dividend (≈£2.4m on £62.3m capex); ~8% gross operating surplus (£150m / 30yr). Own generation — not retail supply, which bankrupted Robin Hood & Bristol Energy.

reinvest_options.label: In-area commercial property for rent
reinvest_options.note: Net commercial yields ~5–10% on property held for rent within the area. NOT borrowing to buy assets primarily for yield — that pattern (Woking, Thurrock) was banned by the PWLB in Nov 2020.
reinvest_options.source: Industry net-yield norms; PWLB lending terms (Nov 2020)

revenue_menu.source: Industry norms; sound vs. PWLB-banned yield-chasing
Lothian Buses (£3.2m div); Nottingham City Transport; 2025 Act lifts the ban

rent_vs_build.source: Illustrative scenario — not a forecast; shows the structural logic of asset vs. liability
rent_vs_build.note: A one-off capital home (built/acquired) becomes a rent-yielding asset; nightly-paid temporary accommodation is a permanent, unsubsidised cost that recurs every year.

council_tax_regressive.label: a £100,000 home pays roughly five times the effective council-tax rate of a £1m home — the tax is regressive and still pegged to 1991 values. So the fairest cuts ask more of wealth and assets while protecting the safety net for those who can't pay.

progressive_cuts.who: Second-home owners — concentrated among the wealthy
progressive_cuts.source: Exeter City Council, Empty/Second Homes Premiums report (2024)
progressive_cuts.note: Premiums are billed by Exeter but shared across precepting bodies; Exeter retains ~8% (~£83k).

progressive_cuts.lever: DIY & construction-waste charges at recycling centres
progressive_cuts.who: Home-improvers and renovators — skew owner-occupier and better-off
progressive_cuts.status: In place; partly constrained by 2024 government limits
progressive_cuts.source: Devon CC chargeable-waste policy; LetsRecycle (Cllr Croad: “people who can afford…home improvements”)
progressive_cuts.note: Soil/rubble £4.10/bag, plasterboard £7.60/sheet; a free allowance protects ordinary household use.

progressive_cuts.who: Garden-owning households — broadly more asset-rich; flats and renters unaffected
Golfers — skew wealthier; distinct from the pool and play areas (see below)
progressive_cuts.source: Exeter Observer (2025); Exeter City Council leisure budget

progressive_cuts.who: Owners of larger, higher-emission and multiple cars
progressive_cuts.value: £35 first permit → £55–£65 extra by emissions band
progressive_cuts.source: Devon County Council, residents' parking permits

progressive_cuts.lever: Means-tested adult social-care charges + deferred payments
progressive_cuts.who: Care users with assets above £23,250 pay more; the poorest are protected by a capital floor
progressive_cuts.source: Devon CC community-care charging policy; deferred-payment policy

progressive_protect.why: The means-tested safety net. Localised cuts since 2013 already pushed bills onto 1.3m low-income households (a ~£630m / 14% real cut) — widely called a “new poll tax.” Cutting it further is the textbook regressive move.

Northbrook pool and 16 play areas were lined up for closure in the same leisure cut as the golf course — but these are used most by low-income families, so they're the regressive part of that budget line.

Cutting socially-necessary bus routes hits the isolated, elderly, disabled and car-less rural poor hardest. The concessionary pass is itself broadly progressive — protect it, don't reframe it as a subsidy to the better-off.
Charging for discretionary spare seats is fair; cutting the core home-to-school entitlement hits rural, low-income families without cars. Keep the entitlement, charge only for the optional extras.
